role_summary: Civil Rights History Project interviewee; his testimony clusters with the Mississippi voter-registration cohort and lands at a near-maximum Collective Discipline reading on the corpus's individual-vs-collective axis.
ai_reading: Conway's top semantic neighbors are Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 76), Barbara Edna Vickers (entry 5), Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan (entry 51), Frankye Adams-Johnson (entry 43), and Robert Bagner Hayling (entry 103, St. Augustine SCLC). The cluster bridges Mississippi voter-registration (McLaurin, Adams-Johnson) and Florida SCLC (Hayling). The catalog's strongest observation about him is on the Individual Conscience vs Collective Discipline axis: he sits at +0.82, very near the Collective Discipline pole and matching the Mary Jenkins (entry 84, +0.83) reading. His four other axis positions cluster near zero (range +0.07 to +0.17), so the collective-discipline pull is isolated, an unusual structural pattern that may catch institutional or organizational testimony content the cultural reading of him would not have predicted.
biographical_paragraph: Purcell Maurice Conway's oral history is held at the Library of Congress as entry 99 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1]. Independent biographical sourcing is pending; the strongest signal currently available for movement role is the embedding pattern visible in the AI's reading above.
movement_context: Conway's interview was conducted in Saint Augustine, Florida, on 2011 September 13, by Joseph Mosnier, as part of the Civil Rights History Project's Florida fieldwork that also captured oral histories from Robert B. Hayling, Audrey Nell Edwards, JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer, and Barbara Vickers [src: 2]. The St. Augustine movement, in which Conway participated as a teenager, ran from 1963 into the summer of 1964 and is documented as a direct catalytic factor in breaking the U.S. Senate filibuster against the Civil Rights Act of 1964 [src: 3]. Local leadership came from Robert B. Hayling, a dentist who served as adviser to the NAACP Youth Council in St. Augustine and organized the city's first sit-in campaigns; Hayling later became president of the Florida branch of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference and issued the invitation that brought King and SCLC staff into the city in spring 1964 [src: 4][src: 5]. The campaign included sit-ins, night marches, and a series of wade-ins at segregated beaches and pools on Anastasia Island and at the Monson Motor Lodge swimming pool, where manager James Brock poured muriatic acid into the water while Black and white demonstrators were swimming together on 1964 June 18 [src: 3][src: 6]. King was arrested on the steps of the Monson Motor Lodge restaurant on 1964 June 11, the only arrest of his career inside Florida; the next day the Senate cloture vote ended the seventy-five-day filibuster against the civil rights bill [src: 4][src: 6]. Conway, then sixteen years old, was among the youth foot soldiers in this campaign. His published recollection describes joining four friends and the activist Al Lingo for a wade-in at St. Augustine Beach in June 1964, where counter-demonstrators carrying Confederate flags surrounded the swimmers, a white youth struck Conway in the face with brass knuckles, and state troopers eventually intervened [src: 7]. He has also recounted that his mother, father, and three brothers were jailed at the same time during the demonstrations, a pattern of whole-family arrests that recurs across St. Augustine testimony [src: 7]. The movement's youth council base, which Hayling had built through the NAACP, is the structural reason a high-school-age participant like Conway ended up at the center of nationally televised confrontations rather than at their margins [src: 5].
legacy_and_reception: Conway's oral history is part of the Civil Rights History Project corpus held jointly by the Library of Congress's American Folklife Center and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture, where the recording is catalogued at accession 2011.174.45.1a-d [src: 1][src: 2]. The St. Augustine campaign in which he participated has been the subject of sustained scholarly attention since David R. Colburn's 1985 monograph Racial Change and Community Crisis: St. Augustine, Florida, 1877-1980, which received the Rembert Patrick Prize and devotes substantial chapters to the 1963 and 1964 demonstrations [src: 8]. David J. Garrow's 1989 documentary volume St. Augustine, Florida, 1963-1964: Mass Protest and Racial Violence, the tenth volume in Carlson Publishing's Martin Luther King, Jr. and the Civil Rights Movement series, compiles primary sources from the campaign [src: 9]. Local memorialization of the foot-soldier cohort in which Conway participated is concentrated at the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument, dedicated 2011 May 14 in the city's Plaza de la Constitucion, and at the ACCORD Civil Rights Museum in the restored Hayling dental office on Bridge Street [src: 5]. Conway has discussed the demonstrations in published interviews tied to NBC's Voices of the Civil Rights Movement series, including a video segment titled Sand, Surf and Segregation that focuses on the beach wade-ins [src: 7].
